claim: AGI will lock value into capital owners who control compute; labor leverage disappears if AI surpasses all knowledge work
actionable: Consider policy implications and societal safeguards now; assume AGI will require UBI or similar mechanisms to prevent permanent underclass

claim: Entry-level jobs are disappearing faster than other roles; recent grads in AI-exposed fields are losing 13% relative employment (Stanford Canaries in Coal Mine study)
actionable: Adapt educational programs to teach AI collaboration, not manual knowledge work; focus junior development on prompt engineering and tool building

claim: Labor demand for entry-level roles is down 15% with 30% more applications per job YoY (Handshake 2025 report), creating bottleneck for new workers
actionable: If hiring juniors, focus on candidates who demonstrate AI proficiency; if job-seeking as junior, emphasize AI collaboration skills

claim: Jevons Paradox: cheaper compute leads to more use cases becoming viable, so total spend increases despite lower per-unit costs
actionable: Assume AI won't solve the economic gap just through cost reduction; systemic changes (UBI, education reform) will be needed

claim: AI is likely only capable of middle-to-middle tasks, not end-to-end; humans still needed for prompting and verification for foreseeable future
actionable: Focus on augmentation workflows where humans handle task definition and QA while AI handles execution

claim: Job augmentation, not automation, is the likely outcome: AI pushes boundaries of tasks, humans still needed on edges but work transforms
actionable: Train teams on job transformation, not elimination; focus on using AI to expand scope and take on higher-value work

claim: Organizations using AI will get more budget for higher-value work, creating new job categories that don't exist today
actionable: Invest in reskilling programs; new roles will emerge (AI oversight, prompt engineering, multi-agent orchestration) that we can't fully predict
